title: Coherent exciton spin dynamics and three-dimensional quantum state tomography in a single InAlAs quantum dot
authors: J. Njala, Y. Yamamoto, R. Kaji, S. Adachi, H. Sasakura
abstract: We investigate the coherent exciton spin dynamics in a single InAlAs/AlGaAs quantum dot using time-resolved quantum state tomography. Under two-LO-phonon quasi-resonant excitation of neutral exciton, we observe pronounced quantum beats in the circular and diagonal polarization components, reflecting the fine-structure splitting ($Δ\approx 19.6 \ μ\text{eV}$). By employing a global fitting procedure across three orthogonal polarization bases, we demonstrate that the spin evolution is consistently described by a unified Hamiltonian dominated by the anisotropic exchange interaction. While the initial degree of circular polarization is limited to $\approx 0.28$ due to fast relaxation processes during carrier cooling, the subsequent dynamics reveal a long-lived spin coherence ($1.1 \pm 0.2$ ns) that exceeds the exciton lifetime ($\sim 767$ ps). Our analysis reveals that the spin-formation time is significantly shorter than the instrument response function, and the absence of a discernible Overhauser shift confirms a negligible influence from the local nuclear environment under the present conditions. These results provide a quantitative benchmark for the three-dimensional reconstruction of spin trajectories using differential polarization signals, demonstrating the feasibility of using quasi-resonant excitation for stable spin initialization in semiconductor nanostructures.
summary: The study uses advanced tomography to map the 3D spin precession of an exciton in an InAlAs quantum dot. It reveals that spin coherence persists longer than the exciton lifetime itself, providing a robust platform for spin initialization and control in semiconductor nanostructures.
main_problem: Achieving a complete, three-dimensional understanding of the spin trajectory of a neutral exciton in a single quantum dot and disentangling anisotropic exchange interaction from other relaxation processes.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated 3D quantum state tomography of exciton spin, revealing a long-lived spin coherence (1.1 ns) that exceeds the exciton lifetime and a spin trajectory that follows a spiral motion on the Bloch sphere.
method: Time-resolved quantum state tomography using micro-photoluminescence in three orthogonal polarization bases (circular, diagonal, and rectilinear) combined with a global fitting procedure.
model_or_system: A single InAlAs/AlGaAs quantum dot under two-LO-phonon quasi-resonant excitation, modeled by a Hamiltonian dominated by the anisotropic exchange interaction (AEI) and a Lindblad master equation.
key_observables: Fine-structure splitting (FSS), exciton lifetime, spin coherence time, initial degree of circular polarization, and the time-resolved spin vector magnitude.
important_parameters: Fine-structure splitting of ~19.6 ueV, exciton lifetime of ~767 ps, spin coherence time of 1.1 ns, and an instrument response function of ~30 ps.
main_assumptions: The spin evolution is dominated by the anisotropic exchange interaction with negligible influence from the local nuclear environment (no Overhauser shift).
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the micro-PL spectrum and FSS determination; Fig 2 displays differential polarization signals showing quantum beats; Fig 3 illustrates the 3D spiral spin trajectory and the decay of the spin vector magnitude.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the experimental setup and spectral characterization, presents the time-resolved polarization measurements in three bases, details the 3D tomographic reconstruction and Hamiltonian fitting, and concludes with the analysis of spin relaxation and coherence.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum optics as it demonstrates high-fidelity 3D reconstruction of a single-qubit-like state (exciton spin) and provides a quantitative benchmark for decoupling intrinsic dephasing from recombination-induced decay.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 2. The differential signals Di (i = circ, diag, rect), de- fined as Dcirc = ˜IL −˜IR (red solid circles), Ddiag = ˜IA −˜ID (blue solid squares), and Drect = ˜IH −˜IV (black solid trian- gles), are shown from top to bottom panels. The intensities Ij are normalized by their respective total integrated counts to compensate for relative detection efficiencies. Pronounced quantum beats with a distinct out-of-phase relationship are observed between Dcirc and Ddiag. In contrast, Drect remains nearly zero throughout the observation window, with its resid- ual oscillation quantitatively accounted for by crosstalk from the other channels. FIG. 3. Three-dimensional reconstruction of the spin po- larization vector S(t). (a) Trajectory of S(t) on the Bloch sphere. The gray sphere shell represents the initial degree of polarization P0, providing a reference for the maximum achievable spin magnitude. The color of data points reflects |S(t)|, as indicated by the color scale. The solid curve repre- sents the trajectory calculated from the global fit (including the IRF) as a guide for the eye. To clearly visualize the pre- cession geometry, the plot starts from the first maximum of Dcirc, thereby excluding the initial rise where the vector ex- pands from the origin.
